sentences.base_language: I signed up for a Chinese cooking class. I'm so excited!
sentences.target_language: 我报名参加了中餐烹饪课，好兴奋！
sentences.romanized: Wǒ bàomíng cānjiā le zhōngcān pēngrèn kè, hǎo xīngfèn!
sentences.grammarNote:
**报名** (bàomíng) = **报** (report/register) + **名** (name) → sign up/enroll (literally 'report your name').
**参加** (cānjiā) = **参** (join/participate) + **加** (add/join) → participate in / join.
Using both 报名参加 is a common colloquial pairing: sign up for and join.
**好兴奋** — **好** (hǎo) used before an adjective as an intensifier (like 'so' or 'really'): 好兴奋 (so excited), 好漂亮 (so pretty), 好吃 (really delicious).

sentences.base_language: Welcome everyone! Today we'll learn to make mapo tofu.
sentences.target_language: 大家好！今天我们来学做麻婆豆腐。
sentences.romanized: Dàjiā hǎo! Jīntiān wǒmen lái xué zuò mápó dòufu.
sentences.grammarNote:
**来** before a verb signals purpose or readiness: 我们来学 = let's learn / we're here to learn. Compare: 我来帮你 = let me help you.
**大家** (dàjiā) = **大** (big) + **家** (family/everyone) → everyone/all of us. Very common in greetings and group settings.
**麻婆豆腐** (mápó dòufu): **麻** (numb/spicy-numbing) + **婆** (old woman/granny) + **豆腐** (tofu). Named after the pockmarked old woman who supposedly created it.

sentences.base_language: First, let's prepare the ingredients. The most important one is doubanjiang.
sentences.target_language: 首先，准备食材。最重要的是豆瓣酱。
sentences.romanized: Shǒuxiān, zhǔnbèi shícái. Zuì zhòngyào de shì dòubànjiàng.
sentences.grammarNote:
**首先** (shǒuxiān) = **首** (head/first) + **先** (first/before) → first of all. A discourse marker used to sequence steps: 首先…然后…最后 (first… then… finally).
**食材** (shícái) = **食** (shí, food/eat) + **材** (cái, material/ingredient) → ingredients.
**最重要的是** = 'the most important thing is…' — a very useful sentence frame: 最重要的是… (the key point is…).

sentences.base_language: Now, let's practice our knife skills. Hold the knife like this.
sentences.target_language: 现在练习刀工。刀要这样握。
sentences.romanized: Xiànzài liànxí dāo gōng. Dāo yào zhèyàng wò.
sentences.grammarNote:
**刀工** (dāo gōng) = knife skill / knife technique. **工** (gōng) = work / craft, combining with other characters to describe skilled techniques: 手工 (handicraft), 功夫 (kung fu / skill).
**要** (yào) here = 'need to / must': 刀要这样握 = the knife needs to be held this way. **要** has several meanings: want (我要), need to (你要注意), will (要下雨了).
**这样** (zhèyàng) = in this way / like this. Contrast with **那样** (nàyàng, like that).

sentences.base_language: Oh no, I cut my finger a little!
sentences.target_language: 哎呀，我手指割破了一点！
sentences.romanized: Āiyā, wǒ shǒuzhǐ gē pò le yīdiǎn!
sentences.grammarNote:
**割破** (gē pò) = **割** (gē, cut/slice) + **破** (pò, broken/torn) → cut and break the skin. **破** is a result complement meaning 'to the point of being broken/torn': 打破 (break by hitting), 撕破 (tear apart).
**一点** (yīdiǎn) = a little bit. Softens the severity: 割破了一点 = just a little cut.
**哎呀** (āiyā) = an exclamation of surprise or mild alarm — like 'oh no!' or 'ouch!'.

sentences.base_language: It's okay! Everyone does that the first time. Here's a bandage.
sentences.target_language: 没关系！第一次都这样。给你一个创可贴。
sentences.romanized: Méi guānxi! Dì yī cì dōu zhèyàng. Gěi nǐ yīgè chuāngkětiē.
sentences.grammarNote:
**没关系** (méi guānxi) = **没** (no/not) + **关系** (relationship/matter) → it doesn't matter / no problem. A very common reassuring phrase.
**第一次** (dì yī cì) = **第** (ordinal prefix) + **一** (one) + **次** (measure word for occurrences) → the first time. **第** turns a number into an ordinal: 第一 (first), 第二 (second), 第三 (third).
**都** (dōu) = all/both/every. 第一次都这样 = everyone is like this their first time.

sentences.base_language: Now heat the oil in the wok. Don't let it get too hot.
sentences.target_language: 把锅里的油烧热，别太烫。
sentences.romanized: Bǎ guō lǐ de yóu shāo rè, bié tài tàng.
sentences.grammarNote:
**把** (bǎ) moves the object before the verb to emphasize what happens to it: 把油烧热 = heat the oil until it's hot.
把 + object + verb + result is the core structure — one of the most important sentence patterns in Chinese.
**别** (bié) = don't (a command). **别太烫** = don't let it get too scorching-hot. **烫** (tàng) = scalding/burning hot (of liquid or surface). **热** (rè) = hot (temperature in general).

sentences.base_language: Add the doubanjiang and stir-fry until fragrant.
sentences.target_language: 放入豆瓣酱，翻炒出香味。
sentences.romanized: Fàng rù dòubànjiàng, fān chǎo chū xiāng wèi.
sentences.grammarNote:
**放入** (fàng rù) = **放** (put/place) + **入** (enter/into) → put into. Directional compound: 放入锅里 (put into the wok), 放进去 (put it in).
**翻炒** (fān chǎo) = **翻** (flip/turn) + **炒** (stir-fry) → stir-fry (constantly turning). Key cooking verbs: 炒 (chǎo, stir-fry), 炸 (zhá, deep-fry), 蒸 (zhēng, steam), 煮 (zhǔ, boil), 烤 (kǎo, roast/bake).
**出** as a result complement: 翻炒出香味 = stir-fry until the fragrance comes out.

sentences.base_language: I made it! It actually smells really good!
sentences.target_language: 我做出来了！闻起来真的很香！
sentences.romanized: Wǒ zuò chūlái le! Wén qǐlái zhēn de hěn xiāng!
sentences.grammarNote:
**做出来了** uses two complements: **出** (produced) + **来** (result arrived) — the thing was successfully made.
**闻起来** = smells like — sensory verbs + **起来** describe how something seems to the senses. The pattern: sensory verb + 起来 + adjective: 看起来 (looks like), 听起来 (sounds like), 尝起来 (tastes like), 闻起来 (smells like).
**香** (xiāng) = fragrant/delicious-smelling. A key food word: 好香 (smells so good)!

sentences.base_language: Wonderful! You're a natural. Keep it up!
sentences.target_language: 太棒了！你很有天赋。继续加油！
sentences.romanized: Tài bàng le! Nǐ hěn yǒu tiānfù. Jìxù jiāyóu!
sentences.grammarNote:
**太…了** = 'too / extremely [adjective]!' — often used as an exclamation of strong emotion: 太棒了 (amazing!), 太好了 (great!), 太难了 (so hard!). It's stronger and more exclamatory than 很.
**加油** (jiāyóu) = **加** (add) + **油** (oil/fuel) → 'add fuel' → cheer on / keep going. The Chinese equivalent of 'you've got this!' or 'go go go!'
**有天赋** = have natural talent (see 有 + noun quality pattern).
